| **Core metaphor** | Read-only dashboard / observatory | Active command center / orchestrator |
| **What it does** | Scans existing sessions, summarizes, shows git status | Launches, monitors, and merges parallel agents |
| **Worktree management** | None (manual) | Automated — creates isolated worktrees per agent |
| **Agent launching** | Resume only (browser terminal) | Full lifecycle — spawn, monitor, review, merge |
| **Code review** | None | Built-in diff review + merge UI |
| **LLM summarization** | Yes (Claude Haiku, background) | No (real-time activity view instead) |
| **Multi-agent parallel** | View only | First-class — designed for running 5-10 agents simultaneously |
| **Auth** | Uses your API key or Claude subscription | Same (your own Claude auth) |
| **Platform** | Web (any OS for viewing, macOS for terminal launch) | macOS native app |
| **Open source** | Yes (Apache 2.0) | No (commercial, by Melty Labs) |
| **Setup** | `uvx agent-kitchen web` | Download app |
| **Target user** | Solo dev wanting session awareness | Team/power user running many agents in parallel |

**Bottom line**: Conductor is an **active orchestration tool** — it's where you go to *do* multi-agent work. Agent Kitchen is a **passive awareness tool** — it's where you go to *understand* what your agents have been doing. They're complementary more than competitive, though Conductor's monitoring features overlap with Agent Kitchen's core value prop.

---

### The Full Competitive Landscape

The space breaks into 5 categories:

#### 1. Polished Commercial Desktop Apps
| Tool | Stars | Key Strength | vs. Agent Kitchen |
|------|-------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Conductor** (Melty Labs) | N/A | Most polished UX, worktree automation | Active orchestration vs. passive observation |
| **Mux** (Coder) | N/A | Enterprise governance, audit logging, OIDC/SAML | Enterprise-grade; overkill for solo devs |
| **CLI Manager** (Solhun) | N/A | Multi-agent support (Claude/Codex/Gemini), no vendor lock-in | Session management vs. session awareness |
| **CodexMonitor** | 3.1k | Rich GUI, remote daemon via Tailscale, iOS WIP | Codex-focused; richer IDE-like features |

#### 2. TUI Session Managers (tmux-based)
| Tool | Stars | Key Strength | vs. Agent Kitchen |
|------|-------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Claude Squad** | 6.3k | Largest community, simplest mental model | TUI vs. web; manages sessions vs. observes them |
| **CCManager** | 931 | Supports 8 different agents, hooks system | Broadest agent compatibility |
| **Agent of Empires** | 1.1k | Docker sandboxing, Rust performance | Security-focused isolation |
| **Agent Hand** | — | Sub-50ms launch, canvas workflow editor | Has a paid tier ($19 one-time / $9/mo) |

#### 3. Monitoring & Analytics (closest competitors)
| Tool | Stars | Key Strength | vs. Agent Kitchen |
|------|-------|-------------|-------------------|
| **ccusage** | 11.5k | Cost/token tracking, billing window analysis | Analytics-focused; no session browsing |
| **Claude Code UI** | 379 | Real-time Kanban, XState status detection | Most similar — web dashboard with richer real-time status |
| **Claude Session Dashboard** | 24 | GitHub heatmaps, cost estimation, CSV export | Deeper analytics/visualization |

#### 4. Orchestration Platforms
| Tool | Stars | Key Strength | vs. Agent Kitchen |
|------|-------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Amux** (Mixpeek) | 71 | Self-healing watchdog, unattended operation at scale | Heavy orchestration; different problem space |
| **Claude MPM** | 83 | 47+ specialist agents, MCP integrations | Workflow platform, not a dashboard |
| **Codexia** | 488 | IDE-like experience, task scheduling, marketplace | Desktop IDE wrapper; much broader scope |

#### 5. Platform-Level (Big Tech)
- **VS Code 1.109+** — positioning as a "multi-agent command center" natively
- **Claude Code Agent Teams** — Anthropic's first-party multi-agent orchestration

---

### Where Agent Kitchen Has an Edge

1. **Simplicity**: `uvx agent-kitchen web` and you're done. No tmux, no worktrees, no daemon, no Tauri build. Most competitors require significant setup.
2. **LLM-powered summarization**: Most tools show raw session names or require manual labeling. Agent Kitchen actually reads session content and generates useful summaries.
3. **Git status integration**: Live branch/dirty/unpushed status per repo group is unique among the observation-focused tools.
4. **Both Claude + Codex**: Many tools are single-agent. Agent Kitchen scans both sources.
5. **Zero workflow change**: Doesn't require you to launch agents through it. Works retroactively on existing sessions.

### Where Agent Kitchen Lags

1. **No active orchestration**: Can't launch parallel agents, manage worktrees, or merge results — Conductor, Claude Squad, and CCManager all do this.
2. **No cost/token analytics**: ccusage (11.5k stars) owns this space completely.
3. **No real-time status**: Claude Code UI uses filesystem watchers + XState for live status detection. Agent Kitchen requires manual refresh.
4. **Single machine only**: No remote agent monitoring (CodexMonitor has Tailscale daemon mode).
5. **No multi-agent support beyond Claude/Codex**: CCManager and Agent of Empires support 8 agents (Gemini, Cursor, Copilot, etc.).
6. **macOS-only terminal features**: The browser terminal works anywhere, but launch-in-terminal is macOS-only.

### Strategic Positioning

Agent Kitchen sits in an interesting gap: it's **too passive to compete with orchestrators** (Conductor, Claude Squad) but **more useful than pure analytics tools** (ccusage). Its closest competitor is **Claude Code UI** by Kyle Mathews, which takes a similar "web dashboard for understanding your sessions" approach but with richer real-time capabilities.

The risk is being squeezed from both sides — orchestrators adding better monitoring views, and Anthropic/OpenAI building session management natively into their tools. The opportunity is becoming the **best observability layer** that works across all agents, not just Claude/Codex.

[build-system]
requires = ["hatchling"]
build-backend = "hatchling.build"

[project]
name = "agent-kitchen"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "Dashboard for monitoring AI coding agent sessions across Claude Code and Codex CLI"
requires-python = ">=3.12"
authors = [
    { name = "Vivek Haldar", email = "vh@vivekhaldar.com" },
]
license = { text = "Apache-2.0" }
readme = "README.md"
keywords = ["ai", "agent", "dashboard", "claude", "codex"]
classifiers = [
    "Development Status :: 3 - Alpha",
    "Environment :: Web Environment",
    "Intended Audience :: Developers",
    "License :: OSI Approved :: Apache Software License",
    "Operating System :: MacOS",
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.12",
    "Topic :: Software Development :: Quality Assurance",
]
dependencies = [
    "fastapi",
    "uvicorn[standard]",
    "claude-agent-sdk",
    "ptyprocess",
]

[project.optional-dependencies]
dev = ["pytest", "pytest-asyncio", "pre-commit", "ruff"]

[project.urls]
Homepage = "https://github.com/haldar/agent-kitchen"
Repository = "https://github.com/haldar/agent-kitchen"

[project.scripts]
agent-kitchen = "agent_kitchen.cli:main"

[tool.ruff]
target-version = "py312"
line-length = 99

[tool.ruff.lint]
select = ["E", "F", "I", "W"]

[tool.ruff.lint.isort]
known-first-party = ["agent_kitchen"]

[tool.pytest.ini_options]
testpaths = ["tests"]

content.text:
Here's Sculptor added to the competitive picture:

---

## Sculptor (by Imbue)

**What it does**: Desktop app for running parallel coding agents in isolated containers with a "Pairing Mode" that syncs agent work into your local IDE/repo.

**Key features**:
- **Container isolation**: Each agent runs in its own container — no git worktree juggling
- **Pairing Mode**: Syncs an agent's container state into your local repo so you can test/edit from your IDE, commit what you like
- **Code quality scanning**: Connects to your codebase and detects issues (missing tests, hardcoded vars, memory leaks, race conditions), then launches agents to fix them
- **Session persistence**: Saves every agent session — plans, chats, tool calls, diffs — so you can resume anytime
- **"Don't come back until tests pass"**: Fire-and-forget mode where agents iterate until CI is green
- **Multi-agent coordination**: Jump between agents' container environments to test and review independently

**Agents supported**: Claude Code today, Codex (added Dec 2025), GPT-5 next

**Platform**: macOS (Apple Silicon), Linux. Intel Mac and Windows coming.

**Pricing**: Free (beta). Bring your own Anthropic API key or Claude Pro/Max subscription.

**Target user**: Professional devs / team leads who want to delegate tasks to multiple agents and verify results before merging.

---

### Updated Head-to-Head: Agent Kitchen vs. Sculptor vs. Conductor

| Dimension | Agent Kitchen | Sculptor (Imbue) | Conductor (Melty Labs) |
|-----------|--------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| **Core role** | Passive observability dashboard | Active orchestrator + code quality tool | Active orchestrator + merge UI |
| **Isolation strategy** | None (read-only) | Containers | Git worktrees |
| **Parallel agents** | View only | Yes, containerized | Yes, worktree-based |
| **Code review** | None | Diff view + auto-issue detection | Built-in diff + merge |
| **LLM summarization** | Yes (Haiku) | No (full session replay instead) | No |
| **Code quality scanning** | No | Yes (detects issues proactively) | No |
| **Session persistence** | Reads existing JSONL | Full session save/resume with plans, tool calls | Real-time only |
| **IDE integration** | None (web UI) | Pairing Mode syncs to local repo | None |
| **Fire-and-forget** | No | Yes ("until tests pass") | Yes (background agents) |
| **Open source** | Yes (Apache 2.0) | Yes (GitHub) | No |
| **Price** | Free | Free (beta) | Commercial |

### Where Sculptor fits in the landscape

Sculptor is the most **opinionated** tool in the space — it doesn't just manage agents, it has its own perspective on code quality (scanning for issues and dispatching agents to fix them). The container-based isolation is arguably cleaner than worktrees since it provides full environment isolation, not just filesystem isolation.

Agent Kitchen's differentiation against Sculptor is the same as against Conductor: **zero-friction observability** with no workflow change required. Sculptor requires you to launch agents through it and adopt its container workflow. Agent Kitchen works retroactively on sessions you've already run however you normally run them.

The risk for Agent Kitchen is clear: tools like Sculptor and Conductor are adding session browsing and history features that overlap with Agent Kitchen's core value, while Agent Kitchen doesn't (and probably shouldn't) try to add orchestration.

Sources:
- [Sculptor: The missing UI for coding agents](https://imbue.com/sculptor/)
- [Sculptor GitHub](https://github.com/imbue-ai/sculptor)
- [Sculptor announcement](https://imbue.com/sculptor-announce/)
- [Sculptor product page](https://imbue.com/product/sculptor/)

message.content: but there is a problem -- agent-kitchen is very much for my workflow that moves between non-git places like my homedir and git repos. both conductor and sculptor are very git-repo-centered. and then they run in containers. i have a giant ~/AGENT folder that contains tons of context that i want applied everywhere

content.text:
That's a real differentiator worth leaning into. The competitors assume:

1. **All work happens in git repos** — but you work across `~/AGENT`, `~/Obsidian_iCloud`, `~/dotfiles`, your home directory, etc.
2. **Isolation is always desirable** — containers/worktrees mean agents lose access to your cross-cutting context like `~/AGENT/memories/`, skills, commands, CLAUDE.md files
3. **Each agent session is scoped to one project** — but your workflow has Claude operating as a general-purpose assistant that moves fluidly between repos, notes, configs, and scratch space

Conductor and Sculptor would actively **break** your workflow. Your `~/.claude/` symlinks into `~/AGENT`, your memory system, your skills that reference `~/Obsidian_iCloud` and `~/Movies` and `~/repos` — none of that works in a container. And a worktree-only view would miss half your sessions (the non-repo ones).

Agent Kitchen already handles this — the `NonRepoGroup` grouping for home-directory sessions, the timeline support for non-repo groups. That's not a gap, it's the product thesis.

The positioning I'd sharpen:

- **Conductor/Sculptor**: "Run many agents on many repos"
- **Agent Kitchen**: "See everything one developer's agents are doing across their entire machine"

It's the difference between a CI dashboard (repo-scoped) and a personal activity feed (developer-scoped). The competitors are building for teams with clean repo boundaries. You're building for the solo power user whose agent is their whole computer.

message.content: there doesn't seem to be that much differentiation between conductor and sculptor. what am i missing?

content.text:
You're not missing much. They're converging on the same product:

**What's actually the same:**
- Desktop app to run parallel Claude Code agents
- Isolation so agents don't step on each other
- Review diffs before merging back
- macOS-first
- Bring your own Claude auth

**The claimed differences are thin:**

| | Conductor | Sculptor |
|---|-----------|----------|
| Isolation | Git worktrees | Containers |
| Code scanning | No | Yes (finds issues proactively) |
| Session replay | No | Yes (saves full session history) |
| Open source | No | Yes |
| Backing | Melty Labs (small startup) | Imbue (well-funded AI lab, raised $200M+) |

The container vs. worktree distinction sounds big but in practice both just give you "agent works in its own copy of the repo." Sculptor's code quality scanning is the one genuinely unique feature — it's not just managing agents, it's opinionated about *what* they should work on.

But yeah, fundamentally they're two teams racing to build the same thing: "Parallel Claude Code with a nice GUI." The moat for either is execution speed and UX polish, not architecture. Which is exactly the dynamic you'd expect in a land-grab moment — everyone saw the same gap (Claude Code is powerful but managing multiple instances is painful) and converged on the same solution.

This actually strengthens your positioning. You're not in that race at all.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Conductor, the Claude Code parallel agents app:

## Overview

Conductor is a Mac app for orchestrating teams of coding agents. It lets you create parallel Codex + Claude Code agents in isolated workspaces and see at a glance what they're working on, then review and merge their changes.

## Key Features

Conductor's workspaces are the heart of how parallel agents work, with each workspace being a separate Git worktree providing an isolated environment for each AI agent to work without interfering with others. Each workspace gets a unique city name, making it easy to identify and manage.

Conductor is a free Mac app that runs multiple Claude Code sessions in parallel, each in an isolated git worktree. It uses your existing Claude Code login, whether via API key or a Pro/Max subscription.

## Demo and Community

For teams using Linear as their issue tracker, the integration connects Linear issue → Conductor workspace → Claude Code implementation → GitHub PR, with the integration demo'd live by Charlie.

There are various community resources and demos available, including a recent Medium article about running 5 Claude Code sessions in parallel and discussions on platforms like Elite AI Assisted Coding about the parallel agent multiplier capabilities with Conductor.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Imbue Sculptor:

Sculptor is the first coding agent environment that helps you embed software engineering best practices. Sculptor runs your code in a sandbox, letting you test code safely, solve issues in parallel, and assign tasks to agents, working alongside any editor.

## Key Features:

Every agent runs in its own container, so they can all execute code safely in parallel. Unlike git worktrees—which share your local environment and require reinstalling dependencies for every agent—Sculptor containers keep your machine safe and let you move between agents seamlessly.

Pairing Mode brings an agent's work from its container into your local repo, keeping your files and git state synced so you can collaborate directly from your IDE. Test the agent's work instantly in your own dev environment and commit changes you like—all without losing your flow.

## Recent Developments:

Imbue rebuilt Sculptor from the ground up. Now you can run multiple coding agents in safe containers and instantly see their changes in action. Sculptor was released back in April as a research preview for catching and fixing issues in agent code.

Sculptor is free while in beta. You'll just need Anthropic access—supporting API keys or Claude Pro/Max plan.

The search results show product information and updates from 2025-2026, though I didn't find specific YouTube video demos in the search results. If you're looking for a specific demo video, you might want to check the Imbue AI YouTube channel directly.

Base directory for this skill: /Users/haldar/.claude/skills/frontend-design

This skill guides creation of distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces that avoid generic "AI slop" aesthetics. Implement real working code with exceptional attention to aesthetic details and creative choices.

## Context Gathering Protocol

Design skills produce generic output without project context. You MUST have confirmed design context before doing any design work.

**Required context** — every design skill needs at minimum:
- **Target audience**: Who uses this product and in what context?
- **Use cases**: What jobs are they trying to get done?
- **Brand personality/tone**: How should the interface feel?

Individual skills may require additional context — check the skill's preparation section for specifics.

**CRITICAL**: You cannot infer this context by reading the codebase. Code tells you what was built, not who it's for or what it should feel like. Only the creator can provide this context.

**Gathering order:**
1. **Check current instructions (instant)**: If your loaded instructions already contain a **Design Context** section, proceed immediately.
2. **Check .impeccable.md (fast)**: If not in instructions, read `.impeccable.md` from the project root. If it exists and contains the required context, proceed.
3. **Run teach-impeccable (REQUIRED)**: If neither source has context, you MUST run the teach-impeccable skill NOW before doing anything else. Do NOT skip this step. Do NOT attempt to infer context from the codebase instead.

---

## Design Direction

Commit to a BOLD aesthetic direction:
- **Purpose**: What problem does this interface solve? Who uses it?
- **Tone**: Pick an extreme: brutally minimal, maximalist chaos, retro-futuristic, organic/natural, luxury/refined, playful/toy-like, editorial/magazine, brutalist/raw, art deco/geometric, soft/pastel, industrial/utilitarian, etc. There are so many flavors to choose from. Use these for inspiration but design one that is true to the aesthetic direction.
- **Constraints**: Technical requirements (framework, performance, accessibility).
- **Differentiation**: What makes this UNFORGETTABLE? What's the one thing someone will remember?

**CRITICAL**: Choose a clear conceptual direction and execute it with precision. Bold maximalism and refined minimalism both work—the key is intentionality, not intensity.

Then implement working code that is:
- Production-grade and functional
- Visually striking and memorable
- Cohesive with a clear aesthetic point-of-view
- Meticulously refined in every detail

## Frontend Aesthetics Guidelines

### Typography
→ *Consult [typography reference](reference/typography.md) for scales, pairing, and loading strategies.*

Choose fonts that are beautiful, unique, and interesting. Pair a distinctive display font with a refined body font.

**DO**: Use a modular type scale with fluid sizing (clamp)
**DO**: Vary font weights and sizes to create clear visual hierarchy
**DON'T**: Use overused fonts—Inter, Roboto, Arial, Open Sans, system defaults
**DON'T**: Use monospace typography as lazy shorthand for "technical/developer" vibes
**DON'T**: Put large icons with rounded corners above every heading—they rarely add value and make sites look templated

### Color & Theme
→ *Consult [color reference](reference/color-and-contrast.md) for OKLCH, palettes, and dark mode.*

Commit to a cohesive palette. Dominant colors with sharp accents outperform timid, evenly-distributed palettes.

**DO**: Use modern CSS color functions (oklch, color-mix, light-dark) for perceptually uniform, maintainable palettes
**DO**: Tint your neutrals toward your brand hue—even a subtle hint creates subconscious cohesion
**DON'T**: Use gray text on colored backgrounds—it looks washed out; use a shade of the background color instead
**DON'T**: Use pure black (#000) or pure white (#fff)—always tint; pure black/white never appears in nature
**DON'T**: Use the AI color palette: cyan-on-dark, purple-to-blue gradients, neon accents on dark backgrounds
**DON'T**: Use gradient text for "impact"—especially on metrics or headings; it's decorative rather than meaningful
**DON'T**: Default to dark mode with glowing accents—it looks "cool" without requiring actual design decisions

### Layout & Space
→ *Consult [spatial reference](reference/spatial-design.md) for grids, rhythm, and container queries.*

Create visual rhythm through varied spacing—not the same padding everywhere. Embrace asymmetry and unexpected compositions. Break the grid intentionally for emphasis.

**DO**: Create visual rhythm through varied spacing—tight groupings, generous separations
**DO**: Use fluid spacing with clamp() that breathes on larger screens
**DO**: Use asymmetry and unexpected compositions; break the grid intentionally for emphasis
**DON'T**: Wrap everything in cards—not everything needs a container
**DON'T**: Nest cards inside cards—visual noise, flatten the hierarchy
**DON'T**: Use identical card grids—same-sized cards with icon + heading + text, repeated endlessly
**DON'T**: Use the hero metric layout template—big number, small label, supporting stats, gradient accent
**DON'T**: Center everything—left-aligned text with asymmetric layouts feels more designed
**DON'T**: Use the same spacing everywhere—without rhythm, layouts feel monotonous

### Visual Details
**DO**: Use intentional, purposeful decorative elements that reinforce brand
**DON'T**: Use glassmorphism everywhere—blur effects, glass cards, glow borders used decoratively rather than purposefully
**DON'T**: Use rounded elements with thick colored border on one side—a lazy accent that almost never looks intentional
**DON'T**: Use sparklines as decoration—tiny charts that look sophisticated but convey nothing meaningful
**DON'T**: Use rounded rectangles with generic drop shadows—safe, forgettable, could be any AI output
**DON'T**: Use modals unless there's truly no better alternative—modals are lazy

### Motion
→ *Consult [motion reference](reference/motion-design.md) for timing, easing, and reduced motion.*

Focus on high-impact moments: one well-orchestrated page load with staggered reveals creates more delight than scattered micro-interactions.

**DO**: Use motion to convey state changes—entrances, exits, feedback
**DO**: Use exponential easing (ease-out-quart/quint/expo) for natural deceleration
**DO**: For height animations, use grid-template-rows transitions instead of animating height directly
**DON'T**: Animate layout properties (width, height, padding, margin)—use transform and opacity only
**DON'T**: Use bounce or elastic easing—they feel dated and tacky; real objects decelerate smoothly

### Interaction
→ *Consult [interaction reference](reference/interaction-design.md) for forms, focus, and loading patterns.*

Make interactions feel fast. Use optimistic UI—update immediately, sync later.

**DO**: Use progressive disclosure—start simple, reveal sophistication through interaction (basic options first, advanced behind expandable sections; hover states that reveal secondary actions)
**DO**: Design empty states that teach the interface, not just say "nothing here"
**DO**: Make every interactive surface feel intentional and responsive
**DON'T**: Repeat the same information—redundant headers, intros that restate the heading
**DON'T**: Make every button primary—use ghost buttons, text links, secondary styles; hierarchy matters

### Responsive
→ *Consult [responsive reference](reference/responsive-design.md) for mobile-first, fluid design, and container queries.*

**DO**: Use container queries (@container) for component-level responsiveness
**DO**: Adapt the interface for different contexts—don't just shrink it
**DON'T**: Hide critical functionality on mobile—adapt the interface, don't amputate it

### UX Writing
→ *Consult [ux-writing reference](reference/ux-writing.md) for labels, errors, and empty states.*

**DO**: Make every word earn its place
**DON'T**: Repeat information users can already see

---

## The AI Slop Test

**Critical quality check**: If you showed this interface to someone and said "AI made this," would they believe you immediately? If yes, that's the problem.

A distinctive interface should make someone ask "how was this made?" not "which AI made this?"

Review the DON'T guidelines above—they are the fingerprints of AI-generated work from 2024-2025.

---

## Implementation Principles

Match implementation complexity to the aesthetic vision. Maximalist designs need elaborate code with extensive animations and effects. Minimalist or refined designs need restraint, precision, and careful attention to spacing, typography, and subtle details.

Interpret creatively and make unexpected choices that feel genuinely designed for the context. No design should be the same. Vary between light and dark themes, different fonts, different aesthetics. NEVER converge on common choices across generations.

Remember: Claude is capable of extraordinary creative work. Don't hold back—show what can truly be created when thinking outside the box and committing fully to a distinctive vision.

Agent Kitchen

Your personal agent activity feed

Not another orchestrator. A dashboard that makes sense of every session your AI agents have run — across repos, your home directory, notes, configs, everywhere.

Open source · Apache 2.0 pip install agent-kitchen Zero config, zero workflow change

The gap

Orchestrators assume your agent lives in a repo. Yours doesn't.

Conductor & Sculptor

C Every session scoped to a single git repository
C Agents run in containers or worktrees — isolated from your machine context
C Must launch agents through their UI to get any visibility
C No view of non-repo work: dotfiles, notes, home directory tasks

Agent Kitchen

AK Sessions grouped by repo and non-repo locations like ~/dotfiles, ~/notes
AK Agents run natively with full access to your memories, skills, configs
AK Works retroactively — scans sessions you already ran, however you ran them
AK AI-generated summaries tell you what happened, not just which file changed

In action

One view across your entire machine

Git repos with branch and dirty state. Non-repo groups for your home directory and notes. Timelines showing work evolution. LLM summaries of every session.

Repos, non-repo directories, session status, and AI summaries — all from one uvx agent-kitchen web

Landscape

Different tool, different job

Agent Kitchen Conductor Sculptor Claude Squad
Non-repo sessions First-class No No No
Access to ~/memories, skills, configs Full Worktree only Container Worktree only
LLM session summaries Yes No No No
Works without workflow change Yes No No No
Repo timeline / history Yes No Session replay No
Parallel agent orchestration View only Yes Yes Yes
Open source Apache 2.0 No Yes Yes
Setup One command App download App download Homebrew + tmux

Get started

One command. Zero config. See everything.

Works with Claude Code and Codex CLI sessions you've already run. No signup, no containers, no workflow changes.

$ uvx agent-kitchen web
PyPI GitHub
17+ tools now compete in the AI agent management space — from Claude Squad (6.3k stars) to ccusage (11.5k stars) to Conductor and Sculptor. Most are orchestrators fighting over the same "run parallel agents in repos" use case. Agent Kitchen is the only one built for developers who use Claude as an everything agent.

# Agent Kitchen **Like browser history for your Claude Code sessions.**

You run dozens of Claude Code and Codex sessions a day. Which repos have active work? What was that session doing? Where did you leave off yesterday? Agent Kitchen gives you a single dashboard for all of it — sessions grouped by repo, LLM-generated summaries, live git status, and one-click resume. ```bash # One command. No install needed. uvx agent-kitchen web ``` ## Why AI coding agents don't have a "recent projects" view. Your session history is scattered across hidden JSONL files. Switching between repos means losing context on what you were doing elsewhere. Agent Kitchen fixes this by scanning your local session data, grouping everything by git repo, and surfacing what matters: - **What's active** — sessions sorted by recency, status badges (done, in progress, waiting) - **What changed** — per-repo timeline showing work evolution over days - **What's dirty** — live git status (branch, uncommitted changes, unpushed commits) - **What it was about** — LLM-generated one-line summaries via Claude Haiku ## Features - **Unified view** — Claude Code and Codex CLI sessions in one place, grouped by repo - **LLM summaries** — one-line descriptions and status classification (done / in progress / waiting) - **Live git status** — current branch, dirty files, unpushed commits per repo - **Repo timelines** — see how work evolved across sessions over days - **One-click resume** — click any session to resume it in a terminal - **Browser terminal** — resume sessions in-browser via xterm.js - **Fuzzy search** — press `/` to filter across all sessions - **Fast startup** — cached summaries load instantly, LLM upgrades happen in the background - **No build step** — vanilla HTML/JS/CSS, zero npm dependencies ## Quick Start ```bash # Run directly — no install needed uvx agent-kitchen web # Or install it uv pip install agent-kitchen agent-kitchen web ``` Opens at `http://localhost:8099`. ## Usage ```bash # Custom port agent-kitchen web --port 9000 # Scan further back in history agent-kitchen web --scan-days 90 # Don't auto-open the browser agent-kitchen web --no-open # Enable background LLM summarization agent-kitchen web --summarize ``` ### Pre-indexing summaries By default, the dashboard shows fallback summaries (the first user message). For LLM-generated summaries, either pass `--summarize` to the web command, or pre-index: ```bash agent-kitchen index # Index all sessions from the last 60 days agent-kitchen index --dry-run # Preview without LLM calls agent-kitchen index --force # Re-index everything, ignoring cache agent-kitchen index --concurrency 5 # Control LLM concurrency (default: 3) ``` Summaries are cached at `~/.cache/agent-kitchen/summaries.json`. ## Authentication (for LLM summaries) LLM summaries require a Claude API credential, checked in order: 1. `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` — standard Anthropic API key 2. `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN` — Claude Max subscription token 3. `pass` password manager at `dev/CLAUDE_SUBSCRIPTION_TOKEN` ```bash export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-... agent-kitchen web --summarize ``` Without credentials, the dashboard still works — you just get fallback summaries instead. ## Configuration | Variable | Default | Description | |---|---|---| | `AGENT_KITCHEN_PORT` | `8099` | Server port | | `AGENT_KITCHEN_SCAN_DAYS` | `60` | Days of history to scan | | `AGENT_KITCHEN_REFRESH_INTERVAL` | `60` | Background rescan interval (seconds) | | `AGENT_KITCHEN_TERMINAL` | `ghostty` | Terminal app (`ghostty` or `terminal`) | ## How It Works ``` JSONL session files (~/.claude, ~/.codex) → Scanner (parse sessions, filter noise) → Git Status (branch, dirty, unpushed) → Cache (reuse prior summaries by mtime) → LLM Summarizer (Claude Haiku via Agent SDK) → Grouping (by repo, sorted by recency) → FastAPI server (JSON API + static frontend) ``` Only interactive sessions are shown. Programmatic SDK sessions (≤1 user turn) and subagent child sessions are filtered out. See [docs/session-formats.md](docs/session-formats.md) for details. ## Development ```bash git clone https://github.com/haldar/agent-kitchen.git cd agent-kitchen uv pip install -e ".[dev]" uv run pytest # Run tests uvx ruff check --fix . # Lint uvx ruff format . # Format ``` ## Requirements - Python 3.12+ - macOS (terminal launch uses AppleScript; the dashboard itself works anywhere) - `~/.claude` and/or `~/.codex` directories with session data ## License Apache 2.0 — see [LICENSE](LICENSE).

## FAQ

**How is this different from Conductor or Sculptor?**

Those are orchestrators — they launch and manage parallel agents in isolated containers or worktrees. Agent Kitchen is an observability dashboard. It doesn't launch agents; it scans sessions you've already run and helps you make sense of them. Use Conductor/Sculptor to *run* agents, use Agent Kitchen to *understand* what they did.

**I use Claude Code for everything, not just git repos. Does this handle that?**

Yes. This is a core design decision. Most tools in this space assume all work happens in git repos. Agent Kitchen groups sessions by repo *and* by non-repo directories (your home directory, dotfiles, notes, etc.). If you use Claude as a general-purpose assistant across your whole machine, this is built for you.

**Don't containers/worktrees break my cross-cutting context?**

They can. If you rely on `~/.claude/` skills, memory files, CLAUDE.md configs, or other machine-wide context, container-isolated agents lose access to all of that. Agent Kitchen doesn't isolate anything — your agents run natively with full access to your environment.

**Does it require an API key?**

Only for LLM-generated summaries. Without one, the dashboard still works using fallback summaries (the first user message from each session). Add `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` or use your Claude subscription token to get one-line AI summaries and status classification.

**Can I resume sessions from the dashboard?**

Yes. Click any session to open a full browser terminal (via xterm.js + WebSocket PTY) and resume the Claude Code session right there — no context switching or copy-pasting session IDs.

**What session sources does it support?**

Claude Code (`~/.claude/projects/`) and Codex CLI (`~/.codex/sessions/`). Each source is scanned independently — if one fails or is missing, the other still works.

**Does it send my data anywhere?**

No. Everything runs locally. Session data is read from your filesystem, summaries are cached locally at `~/.cache/agent-kitchen/`. The only external call is to the Claude API for LLM summaries, and only if you opt in with `--summarize`.

## Requirements
